content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
December
15,
1993
Diane
M.
Fishburn
Olson,
Hagel,
Fong,
Leidigh,
Waters
& Fishburn
300 Capitol Mall,
Ste.
350
Sacramento,
CA
95814
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
A—93-416
Dear Ms.
Fishburn:
You have requested advice on behalf of the California State
Association of Counties
(“CSAC”)
concerning the campaign
provisions of the Political Reform Act
(the “Act”)./
QUESTION
CSAC is considering the possibility of establishing a ballot
measure issues committee.
Will the committee be a controlled or a
sponsored committee under the Act?
CONCLUSION
The committee will be a controlled committee and will not be
a sponsored committee.
FACTS
CSAC is considering the possibility of establishing a ballot
measure issues committee for the purpose of making expenditures in
state ballot measure campaigns on issues which affect counties.
All matters relating to the proposed committee would be controlled
by the Executive Committee of
CSAC,
which is comprised of
15
elected county supervisors who are members of the
CSAC Board of
Directors.
In addition, staff of
CSAC would provide staff support
to the committee.
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000,
q.
All references to regulations are to Title
2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
428 1 Street, Suite 800
•
P.O. Box S07
•
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807
•
(916) 322-5660


Diane N.
Fishburn
Our File
No.
A—93--416
Page
2
It is likely that the principal source of funding for the
committee would be an annual fundraising event.
It is not
anticipated that dues monies will be used to fund or otherwise
support the committee.
ANALYSIS
An organization which receives political contributions
totaling $1,000 or more in a calendar year is a recipient
committee.
(Section 82013(a).)
Each recipient committee is
required to file a statement of organization
(Form 410)
with the
Secretary of State within
10 days after qualifying as a recipient
committee.
(Section 84101.)
The statement of organization must include,
among other
things,
the name of the committee.
If the committee is a
“sponsored committee,” the committee’s name must include the name
of its sponsor.
(Section 84102(a).)
The statement of
organization also must include a statement indicating whether the
committee is “controlled.”
(Section 84102(e).)
Section 82048.7 defines “sponsored committee” as follows:
a)
“Sponsored committee” means a committee,
other than a candidate controlled committee,
which
has one or more sponsors.
Any person,
except a
candidate, proponent,
or other individual,
may
sponsor a committee.
b)
A person sponsors a committee if any of
the following apply:
1)
The committee receives 80 percent or
more of its contributions from the person or
its members,
officers,
employees,
or
shareholders.
2)
The person collects contributions
for the committee by use of payroll deductions
or dues from its members, officers,
or
employees.
3)
The person alone or in combination
with other organizations, provides all or
nearly all of the administrative services for
the committee.
4)
The person,
alone or in combination
with other organizations,
sets the policies
for soliciting contributions or making
expenditures of committee funds.


Diane
M.
Fishburn
Our File No.
A-93-416
Page
3
Section 82016 defines “controlled committee” as follows:
“Controlled committee” means a committee which
is controlled directly or indirectly by a candidate
or state measure proponent or which acts jointly
with a candidate, controlled committee or state
measure proponent in connection with the making of
expenditure.s.
A candidate or state measure
proponent controls a committee if he, his agent or
any other committee he controls has a significant
influence on the actions or decisions of the
committee.
It is clear from the facts you provided that the proposed
CSAC issues committee will meet the definition of a controlled
committee,
because its
15—member Executive Committee will be
comprised of elected officeholders.
Section 82048.7 defines a
sponsored committee as one “other than a candidate controlled
committee....”
(Emphasis added.)
Therefore, the
CSAC issues
committee must organize and file as a controlled committee.
We agree with your argument that the spánsored committee
designation would be a more practical filing status in many
respects for the issues committee of an association like CSAC.
However,
the governing statute does not allow such committees to
file as sponsored committees if they are controlled by candidates.
There is nothing in the Act which would prohibit CSAC from
including its name in the name of the committee for identification
purposes.
If you have any questions concerning this letter, please
contact me at
(916)
322-5662.
Sincerely,
Steven G.
Churchwell
General Counsel
By:
Carla Wardlow
Division Chief
Technical Assistance Division

support the committee.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: CSAC is considering the possibility of establishing a ballot
measure issues committee.
Will the committee be a controlled or a
sponsored committee under the Act?

CONCLUSION: The committee will be a controlled committee and will not be
a sponsored committee.
